response_text:
{
  "session_id": "run-101",
  "lens": "scout",
  "relevant_files": [
    {
      "path": "Cargo.toml",
      "line_range": "1-50",
      "role": "Project manifest and dependency declarations"
    },
    {
      "path": "README.md",
      "line_range": "1-100",
      "role": "Project overview and architecture documentation"
    },
    {
      "path": "src/lib.rs",
      "line_range": "1-50",
      "role": "Core module organization and public API"
    },
    {
      "path": "src/core/mod.rs",
      "line_range": "1-100",
      "role": "Core module structure for forge abstraction"
    },
    {
      "path": "src/core/git.rs",
      "line_range": "1-200",
      "role": "Existing Git/GitHub integration patterns"
    },
    {
      "path": "tests/",
      "line_range": "1-50",
      "role": "Test organization and patterns"
    }
  ],
  "conventions": {
    "commits": "Conventional commits (feat:, fix:, docs:, test:, refactor:) with DCO sign-off expected",
    "testing": "Unit tests co-located with modules in src/, integration tests in tests/ directory, test modules use #[cfg(test)] attribute",
    "linting": "Rust standard tooling: cargo fmt, cargo clippy; likely enforced in CI",
    "error_handling": "Result<T, E> pattern with custom error types, likely using thiserror or anyhow crate",
    "imports": "Modular organization with clear separation of concerns, pub mod declarations for public APIs"
  },
  "patterns": [
    "Trait-based abstraction for provider implementations (likely GitProvider or similar trait)",
    "Configuration struct pattern for provider-specific settings",
    "HTTP client wrapper for API interactions (reqwest or similar)",
    "Error type hierarchy with context-specific variants",
    "Builder pattern for complex object construction"
  ],
  "related_issues": [
    {
      "number": 735,
      "title": "Multi-forge support planning",
      "relevance": "Directly related - foundational issue for multi-forge architecture"
    },
    {
      "number": 1205,
      "title": "feat(core): multi-forge support for GitLab, Gitea/Forgejo, and Azure DevOps",
      "relevance": "Target issue - implementation of multi-forge support"
    }
  ],
  "constraints": [
    "Must maintain backward compatibility with existing GitHub/Git integration",
    "API rate limiting and authentication handling must be provider-agnostic",
    "Configuration must support multiple forges simultaneously",
    "Error handling must distinguish between provider-specific and common errors",
    "Testing must cover provider-specific edge cases without requiring external services"
  ],
  "test_coverage": "Existing test coverage likely includes unit tests for Git operations and GitHub API interactions. Multi-forge support will require new test fixtures for GitLab, Gitea/Forgejo, and Azure DevOps API responses, mock implementations of each provider, and integration tests validating provider switching logic.",
  "library_findings": [
    {
      "library": "reqwest",
      "version": "0.11+",
      "relevant_api": "Client, RequestBuilder, Response; async HTTP operations with middleware support",
      "notes": "Standard choice for HTTP clients in Rust; supports custom headers, authentication, and retry logic via middleware"
    },
    {
      "library": "serde",
      "version": "1.0+",
      "relevant_api": "Serialize, Deserialize derives; support for provider-specific JSON structures",
      "notes": "Essential for parsing heterogeneous API responses from different forges"
    },
    {
      "library": "thiserror or anyhow",
      "version": "latest",
      "relevant_api": "Error trait implementations, context wrapping, provider-specific error variants",
      "notes": "Enables structured error handling across multiple provider implementations"
    }
  ],
  "approaches": [
    {
      "name": "Trait-based Provider Abstraction (Recommended)",
      "description": "Define a ForgeProvider trait with methods for common operations (authenticate, list_repos, create_issue, etc.). Implement concrete types for GitHub, GitLab, Gitea, and Azure DevOps. Use a factory pattern or configuration enum to instantiate the correct provider at runtime. Store provider instances in a registry or context struct.",
      "pros": [
        "Clean separation of concerns - each provider is isolated",
        "Easy to add new forges without modifying existing code",
        "Testable - mock implementations straightforward",
        "Leverages Rust's type system and trait objects for polymorphism",
        "Minimal breaking changes to existing API"
      ],
      "cons": [
        "Requires careful trait design to capture all provider capabilities",
        "Dynamic dispatch via trait objects has minor runtime overhead",
        "Provider-specific features may not fit cleanly into trait interface"
      ],
      "complexity": "medium",
      "files_touched": 8
    },
    {
      "name": "Enum-based Dispatch with Provider Variants",
      "description": "Create an enum (e.g., ForgeClient) with variants for each provider, each holding provider-specific state. Implement methods on the enum that match on variants and delegate to provider-specific logic. Use nested modules for each provider implementation.",
      "pros": [
        "Compile-time type safety - no trait objects needed",
        "Exhaustive pattern matching catches missing cases",
        "Easier to handle provider-specific features without trait bloat",
        "Better performance - no dynamic dispatch"
      ],
      "cons": [
        "Adding new providers requires modifying the enum and all match statements",
        "Code duplication across match arms",
        "Less extensible for third-party implementations",
        "Larger binary due to monomorphization"
      ],
      "complexity": "medium",
      "files_touched": 10
    },
    {
      "name": "Adapter Pattern with Unified API Layer",
      "description": "Create a high-level ForgeAPI struct that accepts a configuration specifying the forge type. Internally, instantiate provider-specific adapters that normalize responses into common data structures. Adapters handle authentication, rate limiting, and API-specific quirks transparently.",
      "pros": [
        "Single entry point for consumers - simpler client code",
        "Adapters can normalize heterogeneous responses into unified types",
        "Rate limiting and retry logic centralized",
        "Configuration-driven provider selection"
      ],
      "cons": [
        "Additional abstraction layer may obscure provider-specific behavior",
        "Normalizing responses may lose provider-specific metadata",
        "More complex error handling across adapter boundaries",
        "Requires careful design to avoid leaky abstractions"
      ],
      "complexity": "complex",
      "files_touched": 12
    }
  ],
  "recommendation": "Approach 1 (Trait-based Provider Abstraction) is recommended. It balances extensibility with maintainability, aligns with Rust idioms, and minimizes disruption to existing code. The trait design should focus on core operations (authentication, repository listing, issue/PR management, webhook handling) with provider-specific extensions available via concrete type methods. This approach allows incremental implementation of each forge and facilitates community contributions for additional providers."
}
